answer:
Definition

SOLID is five design principles that make JavaScript/TypeScript code maintainable, testable, and extensible: Single Responsibility (one reason to change), Open/Closed (extend without modifying), Liskov Substitution (subtypes are interchangeable), Interface Segregation (small, focused interfaces), and Dependency Inversion (depend on abstractions, not concretions).

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the leave approval logic follows Single Responsibility: the LeaveService handles business rules (balance checks, overlap detection), while NotificationService handles Socket.io notifications, and LeaveController handles HTTP routing. Each can change independently — adding email notifications doesn't touch leave logic.

Visual Guide

Code Example

// S — Single Responsibility
// ❌ BAD: One class does everything
class LeaveManager {
  async createLeave(data) { /* DB + validation + notification + email */ }
}

// ✅ GOOD: Each class has one responsibility
class LeaveValidator {
  validate(data: LeaveRequest) { /* business rules only */ }
}
class LeaveRepository {
  async create(data: LeaveRequest) { /* DB operations only */ }
}
class LeaveNotifier {
  async notify(leave: Leave) { /* Socket.io + email only */ }
}

// D — Dependency Inversion
// ❌ BAD: Depends on concrete implementation
class LeaveService {
  private repo = new PrismaLeaveRepository(); // tightly coupled
}

// ✅ GOOD: Depends on abstraction
interface ILeaveRepository {
  create(data: LeaveRequest): Promise<Leave>;
  findById(id: string): Promise<Leave | null>;
}

class LeaveService {
  constructor(private repo: ILeaveRepository) {} // inject any implementation
}

// In production: new LeaveService(new PrismaLeaveRepository())
// In tests: new LeaveService(new MockLeaveRepository())

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Over-applying SOLID in JavaScript. Creating interfaces for every function and abstracting everything leads to over-engineering in a dynamic language.

Mitigation: Apply SOLID pragmatically. In JS/TS, SRP and DI provide the most value. Skip Interface Segregation unless you have large, shared interfaces. Use simple functions and modules instead of class hierarchies.

Definition

SOLID in JS/TS solves the maintenance and testability problem. Without these principles, codebases become monolithic — changing one feature breaks unrelated features, testing requires spinning up the entire system, and onboarding new developers takes weeks.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's ContactsHub, the contact CRUD module initially had a single contactController.ts file handling validation, database operations, image processing (Canvas API), and response formatting — 400+ lines. Refactoring with SRP split it into ContactValidator, ContactRepository, ImageProcessor, and ContactController. This made it possible to write unit tests for validation logic without needing a database connection, and the Playwright E2E tests could mock the image processor independently.

Problems Solved

  • Testability: DI lets you inject mocks — test business logic without a real database
  • Change isolation: SRP means changing notification logic doesn't risk breaking leave calculations
  • Team scaling: Developers work on separate modules without merge conflicts
  • Code reuse: Open/Closed lets you extend behavior (new leave types) without modifying existing code

Visual Guide

Debugging Narrative

In ContactsHub, a bug in the image cropping logic (Canvas API) was causing the entire contact creation to fail. Because everything was in one class, the error was caught by a generic catch block that returned a vague "Creation failed" message. After splitting with SRP, the ImageProcessor threw a specific ImageProcessingError that the controller handled separately — showing "Image upload failed, contact saved without photo" instead of losing all the user's input.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Creating too many tiny classes/files trying to follow SRP perfectly. A project with 200 files containing 20 lines each is harder to navigate than 40 well-organized files.

Mitigation: Group by feature, not by pattern. A leaves/ folder with service, repository, and controller is better than separate services/, repositories/, controllers/ folders with one file each.

Definition

Clean Architecture solves the dependency and testability problem in production apps. Without it, business logic is tangled with frameworks, databases, and UI — making it impossible to change one without breaking the others. Clean Architecture inverts dependencies so that business rules sit at the center, independent of external concerns.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, Clean Architecture separates: (1) Entities — leave request rules (no weekends, no backdating, overlap detection), (2) Use Cases — ApplyLeave, ApproveLeave, GetLeaveBalance, (3) Adapters — Fastify routes, Prisma repositories, (4) Frameworks — PostgreSQL, Socket.io. The business rule "max 2 consecutive weeks" lives in the entity layer — it works regardless of whether the data comes from Prisma, a mock, or a CSV.

Problems Solved

  • Framework independence: Switch from Express to Fastify without touching business logic
  • Database independence: Swap PostgreSQL for MongoDB by implementing a new repository
  • Testability: Test use cases with in-memory repositories — no database setup needed
  • Maintainability: New developers understand business rules without knowing the framework

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Entity — pure business rules, no dependencies
class LeaveRequest {
  validate(startDate: Date, endDate: Date, balance: number): string[] {
    const errors: string[] = [];
    if (startDate < new Date()) errors.push('Cannot backdate leave');
    if (startDate.getDay() === 0 || startDate.getDay() === 6)
      errors.push('Cannot start on weekend');
    const days = this.calculateDays(startDate, endDate);
    if (days > balance) errors.push('Insufficient balance');
    if (days > 14) errors.push('Max 2 consecutive weeks');
    return errors;
  }
}

// Use Case — orchestrates entities and repositories
class ApplyLeaveUseCase {
  constructor(private leaveRepo: ILeaveRepository) {}

  async execute(input: ApplyLeaveInput): Promise<Leave> {
    const request = new LeaveRequest();
    const errors = request.validate(input.startDate, input.endDate, input.balance);
    if (errors.length) throw new ValidationError(errors);
    return this.leaveRepo.create(input);
  }
}

// Adapter — Fastify route calls the use case
fastify.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  const useCase = new ApplyLeaveUseCase(new PrismaLeaveRepository());
  const leave = await useCase.execute(req.body);
  reply.code(201).send(leave);
});

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Over-architecting small apps with Clean Architecture layers. A CRUD API with 5 endpoints doesn't need entities, use cases, adapters, and frameworks in separate folders.

Mitigation: Apply Clean Architecture to complex domains with real business rules (leave approval chains, financial calculations). For simple CRUD, a flat structure with service + controller is sufficient.

Top 3 Clean Architecture Mistakes

Mistake 1: Leaking Framework Details into Business Logic

Importing Express req/res, Prisma models, or ORM-specific types into use case or entity layers.

// ❌ BAD: Use case depends on Fastify and Prisma
import { FastifyRequest } from 'fastify';
import { PrismaClient } from '@prisma/client';

class ApplyLeave {
  async execute(req: FastifyRequest) { // coupled to Fastify!
    const prisma = new PrismaClient(); // coupled to Prisma!
    return prisma.leave.create({ data: req.body });
  }
}

// ✅ GOOD: Use case depends only on abstractions
interface ILeaveRepository {
  create(data: LeaveInput): Promise<Leave>;
}

class ApplyLeave {
  constructor(private repo: ILeaveRepository) {}

  async execute(input: LeaveInput): Promise<Leave> {
    // Pure business logic — no framework imports
    const errors = this.validate(input);
    if (errors.length) throw new ValidationError(errors);
    return this.repo.create(input);
  }
}

Mistake 2: Skipping the Use Case Layer

Putting business logic directly in controllers, making it untestable and unreusable.

// ❌ BAD: Business logic in controller
fastify.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  // Validation, overlap check, balance check, create, notify — all here
  const overlapping = await prisma.leave.findFirst({ where: { ... } });
  if (overlapping) return reply.code(409).send('Overlapping leave');
  // ... 50 more lines of business logic
});

// ✅ GOOD: Controller is thin, delegates to use case
fastify.post('/api/leaves', async (req, reply) => {
  const result = await applyLeaveUseCase.execute(req.body);
  reply.code(201).send(result);
});

Mistake 3: Creating Unnecessary Abstractions

Adding repository interfaces, factory patterns, and dependency injection containers for a 5-endpoint CRUD app.

// ❌ OVER-ENGINEERED for a simple app:
// IUserRepository → UserRepositoryImpl → UserRepositoryFactory
// → UserService → UserServiceImpl → UserController

// ✅ RIGHT-SIZED: Simple service pattern
// userService.ts (queries + business logic)
// userRoutes.ts (HTTP handling)
// Two files. Done.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's CRM Dashboard, the initial codebase had Angular components directly calling HttpClient with business logic inside ngOnInit. Extracting the logic into injectable services made it testable — the sales pipeline calculations could be tested with mock data without spinning up the HTTP layer.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Treating Clean Architecture as a folder structure (entities/, usecases/, adapters/) instead of a dependency rule. The folders are meaningless if inner layers import from outer layers.

Mitigation: Enforce the dependency rule with ESLint import/no-restricted-paths. Configure it so that files in domain/ cannot import from infrastructure/.

Definition

Domain Modeling solves the business logic representation problem — translating real-world business concepts (leave requests, approval chains, sales pipelines) into code structures that enforce business rules naturally. Without it, business rules are scattered across controllers, validators, and database triggers.

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave Management System, the domain model captures real business concepts: a LeaveRequest entity knows it cannot span weekends, cannot be backdated, and must not overlap with existing approved leaves. A LeaveBalance value object tracks remaining days per leave type (casual, sick, earned). The 3-level ApprovalChain aggregate ensures approvals happen in order (team lead → manager → HR).

Problems Solved

  • Business rule centralization: "No backdated leaves" lives in one place, not duplicated in 5 controllers
  • Communication: Developers and stakeholders speak the same language (Ubiquitous Language)
  • Complexity management: Complex rules (overlap detection, balance calculation) are encapsulated in domain objects
  • Bug prevention: The type system prevents invalid states — a LeaveBalance can't go negative by design

Visual Guide

Code Example

// Domain model — pure TypeScript, no framework dependencies

// Value Object — immutable, compared by value
class LeaveBalance {
  constructor(
    readonly casual: number,
    readonly sick: number,
    readonly earned: number
  ) {
    if (casual < 0 || sick < 0 || earned < 0) {
      throw new Error('Balance cannot be negative');
    }
  }

  canApply(type: LeaveType, days: number): boolean {
    return this[type] >= days;
  }

  deduct(type: LeaveType, days: number): LeaveBalance {
    if (!this.canApply(type, days)) throw new Error('Insufficient balance');
    return new LeaveBalance(
      type === 'casual' ? this.casual - days : this.casual,
      type === 'sick' ? this.sick - days : this.sick,
      type === 'earned' ? this.earned - days : this.earned
    );
  }
}

// Entity — has identity, mutable state
class LeaveRequest {
  constructor(
    readonly id: string,
    readonly employeeId: string,
    readonly startDate: Date,
    readonly endDate: Date,
    readonly type: LeaveType,
    private _status: LeaveStatus = 'pending'
  ) {
    this.validate();
  }

  private validate() {
    if (this.startDate < new Date()) throw new Error('Cannot backdate');
    if (this.startDate.getDay() % 6 === 0) throw new Error('Cannot start on weekend');
  }

  get status() { return this._status; }

  approve(approverLevel: number, requiredLevel: number) {
    if (this._status !== 'pending') throw new Error('Already processed');
    if (approverLevel !== requiredLevel) throw new Error('Wrong approval level');
    this._status = 'approved';
  }
}

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Creating anemic domain models — classes with only getters/setters and no behavior. The business logic ends up in services, defeating the purpose.

Mitigation: Put behavior with the data. If LeaveBalance has a deduct() method, the rule "can't go negative" is enforced everywhere automatically. Don't create a separate LeaveBalanceService to do what the value object itself should do.

Top 3 Domain Modeling Mistakes

Mistake 1: Anemic Domain Models

Domain objects are just data containers (DTOs) with no behavior. All logic lives in service classes.

// ❌ BAD: Anemic model — no behavior
class Leave {
  id: string;
  startDate: Date;
  endDate: Date;
  status: string;
  employeeId: string;
}

// Logic scattered in service
class LeaveService {
  canApprove(leave: Leave, approver: User) { /* ... */ }
  calculateDays(leave: Leave) { /* ... */ }
  checkOverlap(leave: Leave, existing: Leave[]) { /* ... */ }
}

// ✅ GOOD: Rich domain model — behavior lives with data
class Leave {
  constructor(private props: LeaveProps) {
    this.validate();
  }

  get days(): number {
    return this.calculateBusinessDays(this.props.startDate, this.props.endDate);
  }

  canBeApprovedBy(approver: Employee): boolean {
    return approver.level > this.requiredApprovalLevel
      && this.props.status === 'pending';
  }

  overlaps(other: Leave): boolean {
    return this.props.startDate <= other.props.endDate
      && this.props.endDate >= other.props.startDate;
  }
}

Mistake 2: Using Database Schema as Domain Model

Letting Prisma/Mongoose models drive domain design instead of the other way around.

// ❌ BAD: Domain follows database shape
// If DB has a join table, code has a join table class
class EmployeeLeaveMapping { /* mirrors DB exactly */ }

// ✅ GOOD: Domain models are independent of DB schema
class Employee {
  private leaves: Leave[] = [];

  applyLeave(request: LeaveInput): Leave {
    const leave = new Leave(request);
    if (this.leaves.some(l => l.overlaps(leave))) {
      throw new Error('Overlapping leave exists');
    }
    this.leaves.push(leave);
    return leave;
  }
}

// Repository handles the mapping to/from DB
class PrismaEmployeeRepository {
  async save(employee: Employee): Promise<void> {
    // Map domain model → database schema
  }
}

Mistake 3: Not Defining Aggregate Boundaries

Loading and modifying deeply nested objects across multiple aggregates in a single transaction.

// ❌ BAD: One transaction touches Employee, Leave, Department, Notification
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string) {
  await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    const leave = await tx.leave.update({ ... });
    const emp = await tx.employee.update({ ... });
    const dept = await tx.department.update({ ... }); // too broad!
    await tx.notification.create({ ... }); // not same aggregate!
  });
}

// ✅ GOOD: Transaction stays within one aggregate
async function approveLeave(leaveId: string) {
  // Transaction 1: Leave aggregate
  const leave = await prisma.$transaction(async (tx) => {
    return tx.leave.update({ where: { id: leaveId }, data: { status: 'approved' } });
  });

  // Event: side effects handled asynchronously
  eventBus.emit('leave.approved', { leaveId, employeeId: leave.employeeId });
  // Notification service handles its own consistency
}

Concrete Use-Case

In Manoj's HR Leave System, the Employee aggregate root manages its own leaves and balance. When a leave is approved, the aggregate emits a domain event. The notification system listens for this event and sends Socket.io messages independently — it doesn't need to be in the same transaction.

Pitfall & Mitigation

Pitfall: Applying Domain-Driven Design to every project. Simple CRUD apps (contact lists, todo apps) don't benefit from entities, value objects, and aggregates — it's unnecessary complexity.

Mitigation: Use DDD for complex domains with real business rules (leave management, financial systems, e-commerce). For simple CRUD, a service + repository pattern is sufficient.
